chief_complaint: worst headache of my life, came on suddenly 2 hours ago
hpi_details: thunderclap headache reaching maximum intensity within 1 minute, occipital and nuchal distribution, 10/10 severity, associated nausea and one vomit, mild photophobia, no focal weakness or speech problems, was lifting at the gym when it started
ideas: thinks he pulled a muscle in his neck at the gym
concerns: wife told him to come in immediately — he's quite reluctant and anxious about hospitals
expectations: wants strong pain relief and to be told it's nothing serious
social_hx: non-smoker, drinks 15 units/week, works as a builder, married with 2 children

reference_soap.subjective: 42M with sudden 10/10 thunderclap headache reaching maximum intensity in <1 minute during exertion, occipital/nuchal distribution, nausea, vomiting, photophobia. No focal neuro deficit. No prior similar headache.
reference_soap.objective: CRITICAL — immediate non-contrast CT head required within 6hrs of onset. If CT negative: LP at 12hrs post-onset for xanthochromia. Neurological exam, BP, pupils, fundoscopy.
reference_soap.assessment: Subarachnoid haemorrhage until proven otherwise — MEDICAL EMERGENCY. DDx: thunderclap migraine (diagnosis of exclusion), RCVS, sentinel headache, benign exertional headache.
reference_soap.plan: IMMEDIATE CT head (non-contrast), IV access, analgesia (avoid NSAIDs if SAH confirmed), neurosurgical referral, LP if CT negative, strict bed rest, nimodipine if SAH confirmed, family notification.

learning_objectives: Recognise thunderclap headache as a neurological emergency
Apply Ottawa SAH Rule clinical assessment
Communicate urgency without catastrophising to an anxious reluctant patient
Explain why LP is needed even if CT is negative
